titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 9 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/94

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
9 XII. 15 My P r. Some nice & ever welcome letters from you. I
am so glad you & Mater have got on so well
together but I am not the least afraid that
you would outstay your welcome or that the
p r would cease to be a very dear 2nd( ??3rd)
daughter to her. I have written to Mr Sclater & said what
you wrote about not writing yourself, & that
"She was is one of those whose simple kindliness has an
influence of which she was probably quite unaware"
& that I had the most grateful recollections of their
goodness to you at the beginning of the war & when
you were sick. I hope to hear tomorrow how your com-
mittee accept the results of your mission, but
unless I am mistaken "some" of them will say
that you have given way too much. I am quite
aware now that there is no animal who
can get her way with tigresses, Mrs Bears
or even b ws like a p r. I can see her
going up to a ferocious tigress — or a tiger still
more I fear ! — & licking her nose & then gambo l
ing like a too active puppy in order to attract
first an amused attention, & at last an attentive
hearing when she sits up & delivers a sermon ! I find it much harder to write to the family
than used to be the case & think I shall have to
go in for a greater extension of buff slips — not to them. Please tell Mrs P KS that I will do what
I can — which is exactly nil — in regard to his
leave. No I will not put myself last for leave
but obviously I don't come first or the p r would
no doubt decide, & it is possible that I might
let Rich.. precede me as a special cae in order to
see his little Jim. For Xmas the best present you can get is
something which you want & which will last till
my return after the war. No there is one thing, no 2 you can get me
a toothbrush a dose of salts either magnesia or
the 2 powders but only a very small quantity. We have
orders to be ready to move at shortest notice & as it is
Douglas may have to deposit some things for me or
I may have to send them home. By the way I have cashed 2 cheques of
F125 + the first one of F 100 since I came out
in payment of mess-bills. I enclose a bill you
may pay if you like sometime but there ought
really to be more discount at any rate another
-/6 which please deduct ! Please make things quite clear to the Dean of Belfast
as you have spoken Lord Farnham is responsible for HQ Units. As the Division is getting out a private kine-
ma ( at £300) which however will probably soon be
repaid, you can see there is no lack of comforts
for the men. If only we could teach them to
think of the future it would be good work, but
the difficulties even for a p r are I fear too immense.
If Gen' Frienddid not remember the p r the b w
would like to eat him indeed. Sorry to hear the Pater is only moderately well. Please Disabuse Mrs G of her idea about
Gen' N being a RC & do it soon or you
will be held responsible. He has been extraordin-
arily friendly to me lately since my litlle flare up !
<I don't think it will last very long.
but I'm sorry to say he seems to be having a ten
dency to snub Russell. I do hope this is not
the case. Very wet yesterday & today & both
days R wanted me to do long motor drives
with him on duty which either one could have done, so
I haven't had much exercise. Richardson as nice as ever but a
litlle gloomy. Always
your b w Wilfrid BSpender
